blocks.statement: I can predict how much diffraction occurs given the ratio of wavelength to obstacle size, and explain why we can HEAR around a corner but not SEE around one.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Long-wavelength radar diffracts around mountains (over-the-horizon radar). Short-wavelength radar doesn't diffract around small objects — better RESOLUTION for asteroid imaging.
blocks.connection: Engineers CHOOSE the radar wavelength based on what they want to detect. Diffraction sets the trade-off.

blocks.markdown:
## Waves bend around things — sometimes

**Diffraction** is the spreading of a wave as it passes an obstacle or squeezes through an opening. The amount of spreading is governed by ONE ratio:

**λ / (size of opening or obstacle)**

- Ratio near 1 (or bigger) → **strong** diffraction. The wave fans out and fills the space behind the obstacle.
- Ratio tiny → almost **no** diffraction. The wave goes straight, leaving sharp shadows.

Sound through a doorway: λ ≈ 1 m, doorway ≈ 1 m → ratio ≈ 1 → sound floods around the corner. Light through the same doorway: λ ≈ 5×10⁻⁷ m → ratio ≈ 10⁻⁷ → light travels in straight lines and you can't see the speaker.

The same ratio limits **resolution**: a wave can't resolve features much smaller than its own λ. That's the radar designer's trade-off — long λ bends around obstacles but sees only big features; short λ gives crisp detail but is blocked by terrain.

terms.definition: The spreading of a wave as it passes by an obstacle or through an opening. More diffraction when the opening is comparable to or smaller than the wavelength. Sound (λ ≈ 1 m) diffracts easily around doorways. Visible light (λ ≈ 500 nm) barely diffracts around everyday objects — that's why we get sharp shadows.
terms.cognate: Sp. difracción · Pt. difração · HC difraksyon

terms.definition: The minimum size of detail you can distinguish. Limited by diffraction — you can't resolve features smaller than ~λ. Radar at λ ≈ 0.1 m can resolve asteroid features down to ~0.1 m. Shorter wavelength = better resolution — that's why X-ray crystallography (λ ≈ 0.1 nm) can image molecules.
terms.cognate: Sp. resolución · Pt. resolução · HC rezolisyon

blocks.title: Diffraction depends on the RATIO, not the wave alone
blocks.markdown: More diffraction when λ ≈ obstacle size. If the obstacle is much BIGGER than λ, the wave just passes straight (light through a window) or stops (light by a wall). The same wave can diffract strongly around one obstacle and not at all around another.

blocks.instruction: Compare the wide slit vs. narrow slit. Which produces a wave-fan? Why? What sets the amount of fanning — the slit size, the wavelength, or BOTH?
prompts: Two panels: plane waves hitting a WIDE slit, then a NARROW slit.
Show the fan (curved wavefronts) behind the narrow slit.
Write the ratio that controls it: λ / opening.

blocks.prompt: Sound has λ ≈ 1 m. Visible light has λ ≈ 500 nm = 5 × 10⁻⁷ m. A doorway is 1 m wide. Compare each wave's λ-to-opening ratio. Which diffracts strongly through the doorway?
blocks.given: λ_sound = 1 m · λ_light = 5 × 10⁻⁷ m · opening width = 1 m
blocks.work:
λ_sound / 1 m = 1          (comparable)
λ_light / 1 m = 5 × 10⁻⁷ (tiny)
Sound: ratio ≈ 1, strong diffraction.
Light: ratio ≈ 10⁻⁷, essentially no diffraction.
blocks.answer: Sound diffracts → you HEAR around the doorway. Light doesn't diffract → you can't SEE around the doorway.

blocks.prompt: PICK A RADAR WAVELENGTH — You want to image an asteroid the size of a city block (~100 m across) but resolve features ~1 m in size. Should you use long-wavelength radio (~10 m) or short-wavelength radar (~0.1 m)? Explain.
blocks.givenHint: Target features ≈ 1 m. Option A: λ ≈ 10 m. Option B: λ ≈ 0.1 m. You can't resolve features smaller than ~λ.
blocks.equationHint: Compare λ to the feature size — resolution requires λ ≲ feature size.
equationOptions: resolution limit ≈ λ (can't resolve features smaller than ~λ)

blocks.title: Different wavelengths, different views
blocks.markdown: Radio telescopes can see HUGE features in galaxies that optical telescopes can't — long wavelengths diffract around the dust clouds blocking visible light. Different wavelengths = different VIEWS of the universe.

blocks.prompt: Why can you hear a conversation around a corner but not see the speaker? Answer with wavelengths.
blocks.frame: Diffraction happens when a wave passes through an opening or around an obstacle whose size is comparable to its ___. The bigger the ratio of λ to obstacle size, the MORE the wave ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **31.1–31.2 and 31.6–31.7** (Huygens' principle, diffraction around obstacles and through openings, laser light, and holograms). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
